mnemonics.title: Early Stopping Reminder
mnemonics.content: When validation loss stops improving and training loss keeps decreasing - that's overfitting! Think: 'Val up? Stop now!'

mnemonics.title: GD Overfitting Pattern
mnemonics.content: Gradient Descent Overfitting = 'Going Down' too far! The more steps down the loss hill, the more you fit the training noise. Remember: 'Down is good, but not TOO down!'

mnemonics.title: Learning Rate Magic
mnemonics.content: High LR = overshoot the minimum (like jumping over a valley). Low LR = slow crawl. Just right = find the bottom without overfitting! Think: 'Goldilocks GD'

acronyms.usage: Step size in gradient descent. Too high = overfitting risk, too low = slow convergence
Number of training iterations. Too many = overfitting to training data
Key indicator - when it starts increasing while train_loss decreases, overfitting is happening!
Penalty term in GD that discourages large weights, preventing overfitting

visualTips.visualization: Draw a wiggly line that perfectly hits every training point but looks crazy/jagged for new data. Training error = 0, but generalization = terrible!
Picture a ball rolling down a hill (loss landscape). If you keep rolling past the bottom, you climb back UP - that's overfitting! Stop at the valley floor.
Two curves: training loss (steadily decreasing) and validation loss (U-shaped). The gap between them = overfitting amount. Close together = good generalization!

analogies.analogy: Like hiking down a mountain in fog - you take steps in the steepest downhill direction. If you take huge leaps (high LR), you might overshoot and go up the other side. If you take tiny steps (low LR), it takes forever!
Like a student who memorizes exact answers from past exam papers but fails when new questions appear. They 'overfit' to the training data (old exams) but can't generalize to new data (new exam).
Like adding a penalty for complexity in a model. It's like a parent saying 'don't get too fancy' - keeps the model simple enough to generalize, not just fit training data perfectly.
Like stopping practice when your performance on mock tests stops improving, even though you could keep practicing and memorize more answers. Better to stop before you over-memorize!
